A few years ago, Dagen Smith and I did an event called the Invisible Funnel Together in DC and I think you guys all have access to it in the inner circle members area. It's an older one, but it was amazing. We had this guy come out and speak. His name's Michael Hague. You were there? Yes. I think your only person was there. Todd was there. Yes, two people were there. It was good? So Michael Hague, we pay this dude from Hollywood to come and speak to us. I told you guys about this. It's called the Heroes 2 Journeys. Him and this guy, Chris Vogler, go through this concept called the Heroes 2 Journeys. He came and spoke at our event and walked us through this concept. For the last 10 years, or 5 years, it's been percolating through my brain and trying to figure out how does what he shared relate to what we do. It took me a long time to bridge that gap, but it's the coolest thing in the world. The Heroes 2 Journeys is based on 98% of all Hollywood films fit into this structure. Michael Hague was telling us, basically, if you look at any movie, from a screenwriter standpoint, you know that every single movie in page X of the script, the hero always does this. On page this in the script, they always do this. It's the same. You can plug almost any movie into this process, which is fascinating. This storytelling and its roots, every movie that's ever become big, all follows this. The majority of all movies, all stories do. He explained it all. I was like, oh, that's so cool, but we're not writing Hollywood movies. How do we actually make that something tangible that we can do? That's the next section. I'll be talking about that. But first, I want you guys to understand this piece of it. Because when you understand this piece of it, then it makes your actual storytelling so much cooler and so much easier. All right. I'm going to go through this piece by piece by piece. The first thing that he talked about is, and here's two journeys, that basically every good story has three core things. There's a character. The character has desires. And then on his way to get those desires, there's conflict. That's it. That's all. Like in his most basic primal state, that is what story is. Three things. Character, desire, conflict. And so, for example, the example he shared when he spoke at our event was Little Red Riding Hood. He said, Little Red Riding Hood, actually the next thing, Little Red Riding Hood has a desire to take a basket of cookies for grandma. But on the journey there, there's conflict. There's a big bad wolf who wants to eat her. That's the story. So that's the plot statement. I'm going to go back. So all the things happened. And one thing he talked about, he said that most people think that the emotion in a story is caused by the desire. Because for you, for the hero, your desire is like, I want that thing. I want to win this thing. See this desire you're going for? That's what causes emotion for you. But nobody cares about the thing the hero's trying to get. What causes the emotion is the conflict in the middle. That's what makes the story actually interesting is the desire, is the conflict. So that conflict is not a good story. So there's always a character, there's a desire, and there's conflict. So again, here's the Red Riding Hood plot statement. So I want you guys to think back about your origin story, how you had your first initial epiphany about what it is you do right now. And I want you to think of it in these three things. There's a character, which is probably you. There's the desire you had, which is to make money, lose weight, invest in whatever your thing is, what's the desire. And then on your journey to get that thing, what was the conflict that you ran into? So all of you guys are now working on writing plot statements, basically this. So if mine was Russell Brunson, I had a desire to be able to make enough money, my wife could quit her job. And the conflict I met along the way turns out it's really hard to build websites. And I had all these issues that came along, right? Super simple. So everyone, let's take a minute and write a plot statement for your origin story, the thing that got you to where right now. Character, desire, and conflict. I had a burning desire to build a business and network marketing. I just felt compelled. It was something I was just aching to do inside, but I didn't know how to be a mom and to be a successful business owner. And what I wanted to bless my family's life started taking me away from them. Cool. That's a good conflict. Awesome. Thank you. Two more shares. Whoa, that was close. The character is my mom, Dr. Jennifer Daniels. I think her desire was to help people or cure people. And her conflict was just the whole medical-industrial complex. And she has to know her real story. It's insane. All right. Nick. The guy's transitioning out of the military, wants to be successful like he was in the service. Little did he know there were nine transition traps waiting to gobble him up. Nice. Cool. All right. So that's the first thing I figured out in any story is the plot line. Okay, is that?
